lesson: L29 — Right-Triangle Trigonometry: Sine, Cosine & Tangent
banner: Week 33 · Right-Triangle Trigonometry — Sine, Cosine & Tangent (SOH-CAH-TOA)
essential_question: Given only one side length and one acute angle of a right triangle — with no way to put a ruler on the side you actually need — can you still find it exactly?
objective: Identify the opposite side, adjacent side, and hypotenuse of a right triangle relative to a given acute angle; define sine = opposite/hypotenuse, cosine = adjacent/hypotenuse, and tangent = opposite/adjacent (SOH-CAH-TOA); and use these ratios — including a calculator's sin/cos/tan keys — to find a missing side.

agenda.title: Launch — The Ladder You Can't Climb

content.prompt: A 20-foot ladder leans against a wall, making a 62° angle with the ground. Without ever touching a ruler to the wall, is there a way to know exactly how high up the wall the ladder reaches?

agenda.title: I do — SOH-CAH-TOA: One Ratio for Each Pair of Sides

content.heading: Opposite, adjacent, hypotenuse — all relative to the angle
bullets: Pick one acute angle θ in a right triangle. The hypotenuse is always the side opposite the right angle — the longest side, and it never changes. The other two sides are named relative to θ: the opposite side is across from θ, and the adjacent side is the leg touching θ (that isn't the hypotenuse).
Switch which acute angle you're looking at, and opposite and adjacent switch too — only the hypotenuse stays fixed.
SOH-CAH-TOA: sin θ = opposite/hypotenuse, cos θ = adjacent/hypotenuse, tan θ = opposite/adjacent.
Given all three sides, sin/cos/tan are just fractions of two of them — reduce and simplify exactly like any fraction (Math-U-See's Lesson Practice 29A/29B).

Given one side and the angle, solve for a missing side by setting up the ratio as an equation and using a calculator's sin, cos, or tan key (set to degree mode) — e.g., sin 62° = x/20 → x = 20 sin 62°.

agenda.title: Explore — Slide the Angle, Watch the Ratios

content.heading: One triangle, one angle, three live ratios
content.note: On the sim, drag the slider to change acute angle θ. Watch opposite, adjacent, and hypotenuse relabel live, and watch sin θ, cos θ, tan θ update both as an exact ratio of the side lengths and as a decimal. Try to land on θ = 30°, then 45°, then 60° — do the decimal values look familiar from Lessons 20 and 21?

agenda.title: You do — Find the Ratios, Find the Side

content.preview: Build session: build or use a clinometer to sight a real angle of elevation, then use sin/cos/tan to find a real height you can't reach with a tape measure.

success_criteria.text: I can identify the opposite side, adjacent side, and hypotenuse of a right triangle relative to a given acute angle
I can state and apply sin θ = opp/hyp, cos θ = adj/hyp, and tan θ = opp/adj to find these ratios as reduced fractions from a right triangle's side lengths
I can use a calculator's sin, cos, or tan key to find a missing side of a right triangle given one side and an acute angle

compass.note: Curriculum/Geometry/The Salas Compass/2026-W33 Sine, Cosine & Tangent.md

sections.title: Find the Ratios, Find the Side

questions.prompt: In a right triangle, relative to acute angle θ, the leg that is NOT touching θ and is not the hypotenuse is called the…
questions.explain: The opposite side sits directly across from θ; the adjacent side is the other leg, the one that does touch θ.

questions.prompt: SOH-CAH-TOA reminds you that cos θ equals…
questions.explain: CAH: cosine = adjacent/hypotenuse. SOH gives sine = opposite/hypotenuse, and TOA gives tangent = opposite/adjacent.

questions.prompt: A right triangle has legs of 6 and 8 and a hypotenuse of 10. For the acute angle θ opposite the side of length 6, find sin θ, cos θ, and tan θ, reduced completely.
questions.answer: sin θ = 3/5, cos θ = 4/5, tan θ = 3/4
questions.explain: Opposite θ is the side of length 6, so sin θ = 6/10 = 3/5; adjacent is 8, so cos θ = 8/10 = 4/5; tan θ = opposite/adjacent = 6/8 = 3/4.

questions.prompt: On the grid, draw a right triangle with legs 5 and 12 and hypotenuse 13. Mark the right angle, choose one acute angle to call θ, and label which leg is opposite θ and which is adjacent to θ.
questions.answer: A right triangle with legs 5 and 12, hypotenuse 13, the right angle marked, one acute angle labeled θ, and its opposite and adjacent legs correctly labeled (either valid choice of θ is acceptable, as long as opposite/adjacent match that choice).
questions.explain: Rubric: a right triangle with sides 5, 12, 13 and the right angle marked (1 pt) + opposite and adjacent legs correctly labeled relative to the chosen θ (1 pt).

questions.prompt: A right triangle has a hypotenuse of 20 and an acute angle of 35°. Use your calculator's sine key to find the length of the side opposite the 35° angle, rounded to the nearest hundredth.
questions.explain: sin 35° = opposite/20, so opposite = 20 sin 35° ≈ 20(0.5736) ≈ 11.47 — remember to set the calculator to degree mode.

questions.prompt: A right triangle has one leg of length 15 adjacent to a 50° angle, and an unknown hypotenuse h. Use your calculator's cosine key to solve for h, rounded to the nearest hundredth.
Exit: a ramp (the hypotenuse) is 40 ft long and makes a 15° angle with the ground. Without measuring, find how high off the ground the top of the ramp is.
questions.explain: The height is opposite the 15° angle: height = 40 sin 15° ≈ 40(0.2588) ≈ 10.35 ft.
